# The Drift — Episode
## Signal, Noise, and the Space Between
Host: Cam
Guest: Jordan Park
#fixture-v3: failure_modes=low_grounding_dialogue,recurring_guest
#fixture-v3: voice=en-US host_voice=en-US

[00:00]
Cam: Welcome back to The Drift. Today we're talking about cultural drift, and I'm joined by Jordan Park, cultural critic. Jordan Park, thanks for being here.
Jordan Park: Thanks, Cam. Great to be back.

Cam: This episode is brought to you by Squarespace. Squarespace makes building a beautiful, branded website actually pleasant. Get started at squarespace.com/podcast.

[03:30]
Cam: Take me through the decision. Where do dialogue and cultural drift intersect?
Jordan Park: Honestly, Culture is a system that never sits still. We've seen this play out at teams using Substack. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Cam: Walk me through a concrete example. What's the structural angle most listeners don't have access to?
Jordan Park: Let me give you the version I actually believe. You can't grip drift; you can only notice it. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.

[12:00]
Cam: We'll be right back after a quick word from our sponsors.
Ad: Today's episode is sponsored by Notion. Notion replaces the dozen tools your team is half-using. Visit notion.com/podcast for a free trial.
Cam: Welcome back to the show.

[14:00]
Cam: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? What's the most underrated piece of cultural drift?
Jordan Park: It comes down to this: Culture is a system that never sits still. We've seen this play out at teams using Substack. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Cam: Walk me through a concrete example. What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Jordan Park: Sure. You can't grip drift; you can only notice it. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Cam: So, like, yeah — that's kind of where I land too.
Jordan Park: Yeah. Yeah. I don't know if I'd put it that way exactly, but close.
Cam: There's something almost — I don't know. Hard to name.
Jordan Park: Yeah. Yeah. I don't know if I'd put it that way exactly, but close.
Cam: There's something almost — I don't know. Hard to name.
Jordan Park: Yeah. Yeah. I don't know if I'd put it that way exactly, but close.
Cam: You know what I mean? Like, that vibe.
Jordan Park: Yeah. Yeah. I don't know if I'd put it that way exactly, but close.
Cam: You know what I mean? Like, that vibe.
Jordan Park: Totally. That's exactly the kind of thing I was getting at.
Cam: So, like, yeah — that's kind of where I land too.
Jordan Park: It depends, right? Like, on the day, on the mood —
Cam: So, like, yeah — that's kind of where I land too.
Jordan Park: Totally. That's exactly the kind of thing I was getting at.
Cam: And you've felt that too, I think? Yeah, you have.
Jordan Park: Yeah. Yeah. I don't know if I'd put it that way exactly, but close.
Cam: You know what I mean? Like, that vibe.
Jordan Park: Totally. That's exactly the kind of thing I was getting at.
Cam: Mmm. Yeah, it's interesting, isn't it?
Jordan Park: It depends, right? Like, on the day, on the mood —
Cam: You know what I mean? Like, that vibe.
Jordan Park: Right? Right. Exactly. That's the thing.

[22:00]
Cam: Let's pivot — systems thinking is where I want to spend the last stretch.
Jordan Park: That's where the conversation gets interesting, because the trade-offs around systems thinking cross-cut everything we've covered.
Cam: That's a useful framing. What's the contrarian angle most listeners don't have access to?
Jordan Park: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Culture is a system that never sits still. We've seen this play out at teams using Substack. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Cam: Walk me through a concrete example. Where do dialogue and cultural drift intersect?
Jordan Park: Yeah, so — You can't grip drift; you can only notice it. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.

[28:00]
Cam: Before we wrap, what's one thing listeners can try this week?
Jordan Park: Pick one of the ideas we covered and run it for two weeks. The rest is iteration.

Cam: And finally, a big thank you to our partners at Squarespace. Squarespace makes building a beautiful, branded website actually pleasant. Check out squarespace.com.

Cam: Jordan Park, thanks for the conversation.
Jordan Park: Thanks, Cam.
Cam: That's it for today's episode of The Drift. See you next time.
